entry.title: Clean Core approach with the ASAPIO Clean Core Check
entry.content:
Introduction 
In this blog, I am going to show how to use the ASAPIO Integration Add-on Clean Core Check as a proactive safeguard to ensure Clean Core compliance – without manually researching released objects, SAP Notes, or successor APIs. 

The idea behind the Clean Core Check is simple: whenever a payload or outbound object is defined, the framework validates whether the used tables or objects are released for usage in SAP S/4HANA (especially in cloud-ready or clean core scenarios). If not, it automatically suggests alternative released CDS views. 

In short: instead of discovering during an upgrade that a table like VBAK is not released, you detect it during design time – and receive a proposal such as I_SALESDOCUMENT as successor. 

This approach requires the ASAPIO Integration Add-on and is particularly relevant in S/4HANA environments where unreleased objects must be avoided. 

 

Why Clean Core matters 

SAP’s Clean Core strategy aims to: 

  • Reduce modification footprint 
  • Prevent direct access to non-released database tables 
  • Ensure upgrade stability 
  • Enforce usage of released APIs and CDS views 

Many classical SAP tables (e.g. VBAK, VBKD, VEDA) are technically available but not released for direct usage in S/4HANA Cloud or Clean Core compliant developments. 

Using them in custom extraction logic or event payloads creates technical debt and upgrade risk. 

This is exactly where the ASAPIO Clean Core Check comes into play. 

 

Example: Payload with classic SD tables 

Let’s assume we defined a Sales Order payload in the ASAPIO Payload Designer (transaction /ASADEV/DESIGN). 

We add the following tables: 

  • VBAK – Sales Document Header 
  • VBPA – Partner Data 
  • VEDA – Contract Data 
  • VBKD – Business Data 

The payload is configured and released. 

Now, instead of discovering issues during a system conversion or ATC run, we execute the Clean Core Check directly in the Payload Designer. 

 

Running the Clean Core Check 

Inside the Payload Designer, simply click on the Clean Core Check button in the toolbar. 

The framework evaluates all tables and objects used in the payload definition. 

 

 

What happens here? 

  • The system detects that VBAK, VBKD, and VEDA are not released objects 
  • It checks SAP’s released API model 
  • It proposes I_SALESDOCUMENT as an alternative CDS View 
  • It flags objects not listed in released content (e.g. VBPA) 

This happens instantly. 

 

Enabling the Clean Core Check  

Before the Clean Core Check can be executed, the corresponding repository configuration must be maintained. 

The check relies on the ASAPIO Clean Core repository, which contains the metadata about released and non-released SAP objects as well as successor CDS views. 

To activate and configure the check properly, follow the official documentation: 

https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1#use_clean_core_checks 

 

Refactoring to released CDS views 

After the Clean Core Check result, the next step is straightforward: 

  1. Remove the unreleased tables from the payload 
  1. Replace them with the suggested CDS view 
  1. Re-map the required fields 

In our example, instead of selecting from VBAK directly, we use CDS View I_SALESDOCUMENT. 

Benefits: 

  • Released API contract 
  • Semantic layer abstraction 
  • Upgrade stability 
  • Cloud readiness 
  • Compliance with Clean Core guidelines 

The payload can now be executed without violating SAP’s release strategy. 

 

Summary 

The ASAPIO Clean Core Check provides: 

  • Immediate detection of non-released tables 
  • Automatic suggestion of released CDS alternatives 
  • Support for SAP Clean Core strategy 
  • Reduced upgrade risks 
  • Faster development cycles 

Instead of reacting to ATC findings during system conversion, developers can proactively design compliant payloads from the beginning. 

It shifts Clean Core validation directly into the design phase. 

In other words: 

Configuration-driven integration, now also Clean Core compliant. 

Let me know if you would like to see a deeper technical walkthrough or how the check is implemented internally in the Add-on.

entry.title: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I have seen a number of implementations of event-driven design involving SAP Event Mesh and the AMQP sender adapter on Cloud Integration. Most were built on the incorrect assumption that standard settings would be sufficient — without proper end-to-end testing to validate that assumption.

In this blog, I explain two patterns I have used in practice, along with their strengths and trade-offs. I have also added an important baseline section on why the native AMQP retry configuration alone is not enough.

Finally, we will look at whether Advanced Event Mesh requires the same workarounds — or whether it solves the problem out of the box.

Baseline: Why Native AMQP Retry Is Not Enough

The AMQP sender adapter does have a built-in Retry Details configuration (Max. Number of Retries + Delivery Status After Max. Retries). However, the native retry has a critical limitation: there is no configurable retry delay or backoff interval. The broker redelivers immediately and — if not set up correctly — indefinitely. This means a temporary downstream outage produces a burst of failures rather than graceful back-pressure.

Max Redelivery Count in Event Mesh queue configuration set as as EMPTY/0 or Max. Number of Retries in the AMQP sender adapter set as EMPTY results in indefinite, immediate redeliveries that flood both the EM queue and the CI tenant — a serious stability risk.

📝Remark: According to SAP Note 3269414, configuring a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) in Event Broker can help limit retries, but this is not affecting Event Mesh.

The practical hint: to effectively limit number of retries you need to set retry number > 0 in Event Mesh queue settings and AMQP adapter.

JMSXDeliveryCount header is auto-incremented only for internal AMQP retries, not overall number of retries (AMQP + Broker side). JMSRedelivered header switch from false to true. Therefore retry strategy cannot be designed inside Iflow similary to JMS (SAPJMSRetries header). A potential workaround is to use a custom header (please see the description in Option 2).

As you can see, we can limit the number of retries, but we can't set a solid retry interval. That's exactly why I wrote this blog post with two solutions.

Each pattern has its strengths and trade-offs. Let's dive in.

Option 1: Decouple AMQP from iFlow Logic via JMS

In my previous blog series on JMS, I compared whether to use JMS or an Event Broker directly:
JMS Mastery Series Part 3 — JMS in Production

Actually, both approaches can work in synergy for certain scenarios — retry handling being a prime example.

The core idea: use the AMQP adapter only to ingest the message from Event Mesh, immediately write it to a JMS queue, and let the JMS adapter handle all retry logic from that point forward. Event Mesh is decoupled from the processing logic as soon as the message lands in JMS. The iFlow finishes with COMPLETED status and the message is acknowledged and removed from the EM queue.

How it works

  • The AMQP sender adapter receives the message from Event Mesh.
  • The iFlow immediately writes the payload to a JMS queue, which acts as an internal buffer.
  • A second iFlow reads from JMS and executes the full processing logic.
  • JMS handles retries natively with a configurable interval and exponential backoff.

Pros

  • The Event Mesh connection is short-lived — released immediately after the JMS write, freeing EM resources.
  • Full JMS retry capability: configurable retry interval, exponential backoff, and maximum retry interval — none of which are available in the AMQP adapter.
  • Proven pattern — behaves identically to any JMS-backed HTTP or SOAP integration.

Cons

  • Consumes additional JMS resources — two queues are involved: the external EM queue and the internal JMS queue.
  • Increased operational overhead: more moving parts to monitor and maintain.

To reduce the number of JMS queues, you can assign one JMS queue per group of interfaces, or even one JMS queue for all interfaces from a single Event Mesh instance. However, this breaks the 1:1 mapping between EM queues and JMS queues, with important consequences.

💡Personal note: Even as a JMS advocate, I apply this pattern selectively. It is not the default. I add JMS on top of AMQP only when the iFlow logic is genuinely heavy — complex mappings, multi-step orchestration with high processing time or calls to multiple external systems (Recipient List pattern). Second use case is when you truly need robust retry control with exponential backoff. For operationally lightweight flows, Option 2 is cleaner.

Option 2: Retry Queue + TTL + DMQ (No JMS)

This pattern implements a retry-with-delay mechanism entirely within Event Mesh, using a dedicated Retry queue and TTL expiration to simulate retry backoff — without involving JMS at all.

How it works

  • The main iFlow catches errors in the exception subprocess.
  • If the error is transient and eligible for retry, the message is forwarded to a dedicated Retry queue in Event Mesh via an AMQP receiver adapter.
  • The Retry queue is configured with: DMQ = original processing queue, Max TTL = desired retry delay in seconds.
  • When the TTL expires, Event Mesh automatically moves the message to the DMQ — which is the original queue — and processing restarts.
  • The iFlow ends with an End Message event (COMPLETED status), so no automatic AMQP retry is triggered.

The TTL value effectively becomes your retry delay interval. You can implement increasing delays by chaining multiple retry queues with different TTL values, though this adds configuration complexity.

This creates a controlled retry loop: the message cycles between the Retry queue and the original queue until it either succeeds or the retry counter threshold is reached.

Tracking retry count

Because JMSXDeliveryCount resets to 1 and JMSRedelivered resets to false on every queue hop — this is a new delivery, not a broker redelivery — you cannot rely on these headers to count retry attempts.

Two approaches for maintaining a retry counter:

  • Custom header approach: add a RetryCount header to the message before forwarding it to the Retry queue, increment it on each pass, and stop retrying when it exceeds your threshold. This avoids any persistence layer and is the preferred approach for most scenarios.
  • Data Store approach: persist a counter keyed on a message correlation ID. This can introduce read/write lock contention at high throughput.

Pros

  • No JMS resources consumed.
  • Retry delay is cleanly controlled via TTL — no thread blocking.

Cons

  • The setup is non-obvious and requires careful EM queue configuration.
  • JMSXDeliveryCount and JMSRedelivered are unreliable for retry counting — custom counter logic is required.
  • A Data Store-based counter introduces contention risk at high throughput.
  • No native exponential backoff — fixed TTL only, unless you chain queues.

💡Personal note: This is my preferred option. Clean, JMS-free, and elegant once you understand the TTL mechanics.

Option 3 (Not Recommended): Groovy Sleep as Retry Delay

This is a workaround I have seen in the field: using a sleep() call inside a Groovy script to introduce a delay before retrying, relying on the AMQP adapter's built-in retry to re-execute the iFlow.

Pros

  • No additional infrastructure — no JMS, no Retry queue, no TTL configuration.
  • Simple to implement for a quick proof of concept.

Cons

  • The AMQP connection to Event Mesh remains open and locked for the entire sleep duration, preventing EM from reclaiming the resource.
  • The CI worker thread is blocked for the sleep duration. Under concurrent load, this exhausts the worker thread pool and degrades performance for all iFlows on the same tenant — not just this one.
  • sleep() in Groovy on Cloud Integration is a tenant-level anti-pattern. At scale, it can cause cascading timeouts across unrelated integrations. This is not merely an aesthetic concern — it is a production stability risk.
  • No retry limit control without additional explicit design.

💡Personal note: Even though it is simple to implement, never use this in production. It is a quick hack, but the connection lock makes it an anti-pattern for any scenario requiring scalability. I would not recommend it to anyone.

Pattern Comparison

Use this table to guide your pattern selection:

CriteriaOption 1: JMS BridgeOption 2: TTL + DMQOption 3: Groovy SleepAMQP Retry
JMS RequiredYesNoNoNo
Retry Delay Control✅Full — retry interval, exponential backoff, max retry interval✅Via TTL — retry interval only, no exponential backoff⚠️Via sleep command only❌Immediate redelivery only
Retry CounterSAPJMSRetries headerCustom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data StoreEM + AMQP settings or Custom header / Data Store
EM Connection DurationShort - decoupled from iFlow processingStandard - but prevents long-lived connections caused by retriesLong ⚠️Standard
High Throughput FitYes (with care)Limited if Data Store used as counterNoYes (with care)
Setup ComplexityMediumMediumLowLow
Recommended ForHeavy iFlow logic, strict retry needsMost event-driven cases — low-to-moderate iFlow logic, flat retry interval is sufficientNot recommended — development/POC use only at mostSimple flows only — needs redesign for production

Does Retrying in Advanced Event Mesh Work Out of the Box?

Short answer: yes — AEM retry logic works like a charm. This is not surprising given that Advanced Event Mesh is not an entirely new product, but rather a white-label of Solace PubSub+, a very mature messaging solution with years of production use behind it.

What Can We Expect from AEM and the AEM Sender Adapter in Cloud Integration?

The full spectrum of retry configuration is available directly at the adapter level:

  • Retry interval
  • Maximum retry interval
  • Exponential backoff multiplier
  • Maximum number of retries

All of the above are configured directly on the AEM adapter in Cloud Integration, and all work as expected. No surprises, no disappointments. It is the best of both worlds:

  • JMS-level retry interval configuration is available
  • Event Mesh-level retry limit configuration is available

AEM offers not only adapter-level retries (CI side) but also broker-level retries (Max Redelivery Count on the queue). After CI-side retries are exhausted, a failed status is sent back to AEM, which then redelivers the message according to its own Max Redelivery Count — so the total retry attempts is a product of both settings, not a simple sum. Factor this in when configuring both to avoid unexpectedly high total retry counts.
This works like described above if Acknowledgment mode is set to: Automatic On Exchange Completed and Settlement outcome: Failed.

If you are fortunate enough to be working with AEM on your project, you do not need to apply any of the workarounds described above or add JMS on top of your event-driven design purely for the sake of working retry logic.

Final Thoughts

These two patterns exist because the AMQP adapter's native retry has no configurable delay. Until SAP adds native retry backoff to the AMQP adapter — or Event Mesh provides broker-side delay queues as a first-class feature — these workarounds remain necessary for production-grade event-driven designs.

My default recommendations:

  • Start with Option 2 (TTL + DMQ) for most flows. It is clean, JMS-free, and operationally lightweight.
  • Escalate to Option 1 (JMS bridge) only when the iFlow processing logic is genuinely complex or you need exponential backoff.
  • Never use the Groovy Sleep approach in production.
  • If AEM is available, the standard adapter configuration gives you a complete, production-grade solution out of the box — no workarounds required.

Additional Notes for Production

Idempotency and duplicate handling: Complement your chosen retry pattern with idempotency handling to avoid duplicate processing in the target system. The best place to implement deduplication is the receiver application, not the middleware. Also note that the event broker does not guarantee exactly-once processing — you must design for duplicates and assume at-least-once delivery.

Define the final stop after retries: Always define what happens when retries are exhausted. Route the message to a Dead Message Queue (DMQ) or equivalent quarantine mechanism, and ensure there is an operational process for monitoring and reprocessing those messages.

Are you using any of these patterns? Have you found other approaches? Comments, questions, and observations are very welcome.

entry.title: Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenario Part - 3
entry.content:
In Part 1, I explained the overall setup of B2B Advisory. How the TPM foundation was structured. With the Company, Trading Partner, and Systems configuration in place, the next critical layer is the Agreement.

In Part 2, I explained the setup of the agreement. How the agreemetn can help to identigy the business process along with the trading partner identifer how that works.

In this final part I will explain how the standard flow works and what customization I did to fullfill my requriement (integration with custom EDI XML).

Information about the standard EDI Integration package

Package name : Cloud Integration - Trading Partner Management V2 (can be found in discovery)

There are multiple option of the trigger available based on how the actual call trigger for example in this we have the iflow for AS2, AS2 MDN, IDoc, Process Direct and SOAP. All of them are exposed with endpoints which can be used as per the requirement. 

Other then these iflow we have several other Iflows which are being used to process data as per the EDI File type along with to execute the business process defined in the agreements.

As my interface is the inbound interface where from one of the SFTP file needs to pick and IDoc ORDERS.ORDERS05. To complete this required I have create

flow 1 : which pick the file from the SFTP and parse/create the required headers which are essential for processing in further step.

the pasring steps is being handled by the 2nd flow Step 1b - write Message to Message Queue which I have modifed with customized XSLT and header, which support my configuration done while creating agreement (identifier for receiver).

The names of the headers I choose are not random, because in next IFlow where agreement identification are being executed, it picked these identifiers from the headers only to create agreement plain id. I have already mentioned the attributes of agreement plain ID and below are the headers list for the same.

OrderField in Match StringScript VariableHeader SourceCustomized
1Adapter TypesndAdapterTypeSAP_COM_SND_Adapter_Typecustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
2Document StandardsndDocumentStandardSAP_EDI_Document_Standardcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
3Message NamespacesndMessageNamespaceSAP_EDI_Message_Namespace 
4Message VersionsndMessageVersionSAP_EDI_Message_Versioncustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
5Sender IDsndSenderIdSAP_EDI_Sender_IDcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
6Sender ID QualifiersndSenderIdQualifierSAP_EDI_Sender_ID_Qualifiercustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
7Sender Partner TypesndSenderPartnerTypeSAP_EDI_Sender_Partner_Type 
8Sender System IDsndSenderSystemIdSAP_EDI_GS_Sender_ID (later overwritten to "") 
9Sender System ID QualifiersndSenderSystemIdQualifierSAP_EDI_GS_Sender_ID_Qualifier 
10Receiver IDsndReceiverIdSAP_EDI_Receiver_IDcustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
11Receiver ID QualifiersndReceiverIdQualifierSAP_EDI_Receiver_ID_Qualifiercustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)
12Receiver Partner TypesndReceiverPartnerTypeSAP_EDI_Receiver_Partner_Type 
13Receiver System IDsndReceiverSystemIdSAP_EDI_GS_Receiver_ID (later overwritten to "") 
14Receiver System ID QualifiersndReceiverSystemIdQualifierSAP_EDI_GS_Receiver_ID_Qualifier 
15Message TypesndMessageTypeSAP_EDI_Message_Typecustomized standard flow externlised parameter (headers)

Groovy analysis : In case you have come accross the script "callPDwithHeaderParameters.groovy" you might seen the line where "PidStringTemplate" is being created with certain variables which are driven by the header mentioned above with some additional placeholder "%s" which further being replaced with "sndSenderId" and "sndReceiverId".

// pidStrintTemplate
def PidStringTemplate = sndAdapterType + "-" + sndDocumentStandard + "-" + sndMessageNamespace + "-" + sndMessageVersion + "-%s-" + sndSenderIdQualifier + "-" + sndSenderPartnerType + "-" + sndSenderSystemId + "-" + sndSenderSystemIdQualifier + "-%s-" + sndReceiverIdQualifier + "-" + sndReceiverPartnerType + "-" + sndReceiverSystemId + "-" + sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier + "-" + sndMessageType;

// placing senderID and receiverID for placeholders %s
def defaultPid = String.format(PidStringTemplate, sndSenderId, sndReceiverId)

// creating parter ID plain string and md5 hash header
message.setHeader("ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID", partnerID);
message.setHeader("SAP_Partner_ID_MATCH_String", matchString);
message.setHeader("SAP_TPM_ACTIVITYPARTNER_ID", partnerID);

These are the complete steps which need to be performed for customized EDIFacts processing and what I did to achive my requriements.

 

Additional resource

Some times for partner based value mapping, we mainted the general data inside the transactions of an agreement, which can help during the dynamic field data mapping. to pick these values from agreement below code can be utilized. This code get the value from one of the header "ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID" which can be use to lookup inside the partnerDirectory data. All the general data we maintain under Activity paramters (agreement transaction) are stored under the "Binary parameter" with the ID "SAP_TPM_CustomActivityParams" in the form of JSon.

import com.sap.it.api.pd.PartnerDirectoryService;
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory;
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import groovy.json.*;
import com.sap.it.api.pd.BinaryData;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def pdService = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null)
    if (pdService == null) throw new Exception("Partner Directory Service not available")
    def partnerID = message.getHeaders().get("ACTUAL_PARTNER_ID");

    if (partnerID != null) {
        // dynamic parameters for custom activity
		def customActivityParams = pdService.getParameter("SAP_TPM_CustomActivityParams", partnerID, BinaryData.class);
		if (customActivityParams != null){
			def customActivityParamsStr = new String(customActivityParams.getData());
			def customActivityParamsJson = new JsonSlurper().parseText(customActivityParamsStr);
			customActivityParamsJson.each{
				paramKey, paramValue -> message.setProperty(paramKey, paramValue);
			}
		}
    }
    return message
}

Apart from these sometime complex value map can be maintained under Manager Partner Directory (under Manage Stores) which generally can be without partner specific but may be based on the Counrty, plant etc. This can be access with below code.

import com.sap.it.api.pd.PartnerDirectoryService
import com.sap.it.api.ITApiFactory
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {
	
    // 1. Initialize Partner Directory Service
    def pdService = ITApiFactory.getApi(PartnerDirectoryService.class, null)
    if (pdService == null) throw new Exception("Partner Directory Service not available")

    // 2. Handle First PID (Suffix logic)
    // Assuming input PID comes from a header or property named 'Input_PID'
    String inputPid = message.getHeaders().get("PD_APID") ?: message.getProperties().get("PD_APID")
    
	if (inputPid) {
        String pid1 = inputPid + "_PD"
        String val1 = pdService.getParameter("SNDPRN", pid1, String.class)
		String val2 = pdService.getParameter("SNDPRT", pid1, String.class)
        message.setProperty("Partner_SNDPRN", val1 ?: "") // Empty if null
		message.setProperty("Partner_SNDPRT", val2 ?: "") // Empty if null
    }

    // 3. Handle Second PID (EDI_DC40_QA)
    String pid2 = message.getHeaders().get("SAP_ID") ?: message.getProperties().get("SAP_ID")
    
	// These can be changed based on the requirement and ID's which maintained uder Partner Directory
	def paramList = ["SNDPOR", "RCVPOR", "RCVPRT", "RCVPRN"]

    paramList.each { paramId ->
        String value = pdService.getParameter(paramId, pid2, String.class)
        // Store in property named 'Partner_SNDPRT', 'Partner_SNDPOR', etc.
        message.setProperty("Partner_" + paramId, value ?: "")
    }
    return message
}

 

Thanks

Aman Sharma

entry.title: Your Blueprint to Lead the Shift in SAP S/4HANA Utilities
entry.content:
The Utilities industry is moving fast toward cloud-ready architectures, customer-first operations, and intelligent,  AI-enabled processes. If you're an architect, business user , or consultant, you're at the center of this transformation.  

The journey begins with a thorough introduction to SAP S/4HANA Utilities, covering unique system capabilities, end-to-end industry processes, and customer service operations. From there, you will explore the strategic differences between SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA Utilities, learning to evaluate conversion options and apply proven transition methodologies using tools like SAP Readiness Check. 

The curriculum then guides you through essential system configuration, including master data modeling, device management with inventory and procurement processes, meter reading, and integration with other SAP components. Billing and invoicing are addressed in depth, from rate structure configuration to budget billing plans and exception handling. 

For the German energy market, specialized training covers market communication processes, including the cloud-based SAP Market Communication (MaCo) solution, Application Process Engine (APE), and the integration into SAP S/4HANA Utilities with a focus on regulatory compliance and automation. 

Finally, the curriculum prepares you to build future-proof architectures by keeping the SAP S/4HANA core clean through extensibility on SAP Business Technology Platform, and utilizing SAP Fiori for a modern, industry-specific user experience. 

Ready to go hands on? 

Build end-to-end skills with the refreshed course stack:  

  1. S4IUT is your overview and entry point to the SAP S/4HANA for Utilities. The course covers  the business processes , Fiori-based functions, AI-enabled optimization, asset management architecture, and cloud integrations: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/executing-business-processes-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  2. IUT21 focuses on master data and basic functions configuration: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-master-data-and-basic-functions-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  3. IUT22 deep-dives into device management configuration, from master data and inventory to installations, inspections, and meter reading: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-device-management-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  4. IUT23 covers billing and invoicing configuration, including rate structures, execution, exceptions, and budget billing: https://learning.sap.com/courses/configuring-billing-and-invoicing-in-sap-s-4hana-utilities 
  5. If you operate in the German energy market, IUT51 explores market communication (MaCo-Cloud) with the Application Process Engine (APE): https://learning.sap.com/courses/explore-sap-market-communication-for-utilities-in-the-german-energy-market 
  6. Round it out with the updated S4IUD gives you the strategic and technical insights to guide customers from SAP ECC to SAP S/4HANA with confidence: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/transitioning-to-sap-s-4hana-for-utilities 

Ready to lead your customers’ transition with clarity and impact? Start with S4IUT (or S4IUD), then progress through the updated stack to build execution and configuration expertise.

entry.title: Step by step guide to creating your first iFlow in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Introduction

If you are new to SAP Cloud Integration, creating your first iFlow may feel a little overwhelming. But don't worry once you understand the basic building blocks (Sender, Steps, Receiver), and how to deploy and monitor you will be able to build real-world integrations confidently.

In this blog, I will walk you through a beginner-friendly iFlow that exposes a HTTP endpoint and returns a simple response - the fastest way to understand the full lifecycle: design --> deploy --> test --> monitor.

What you will learn

By the end of this blog, you will know how to:

  • Create integration package
  • Create and design iFlow
  • Configure HTTPS Sender
  • Use a content modifier to set a response payload
  • Deploy an iflow
  • Test the endpoint using Postman
  • Monitor the message processing 

Prerequisites

Before starting you should have:

  • Access to SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration capability)
  • Necessary authorizations to create and deploy artifacts (at least integration developer permissions)

Scenario Overview

We will create an iFlow that:

  • Receives a request via HTTPS
  • Sets a response message: "Hello SAP CPI"
  • returns it back to the caller (synchronous)

Step by step guide: Build your first iFlow

Step 1: Go to integration flow designer

  • Open 'SAP Integration Suite'.
  • Navigate to: Design --> Integrations and APIs

  •  

Step 2: Create an Integration Package

  • Click 'Create'.
  • Provide: Name and Short Description

  •  

  • Click "Save".

Step 3: Create an Integration Flow(iFlow)

  • Open your package --> Go to 'Artifacts' tab.
  • Click on 'Add' and select 'Integration flow'

  •  

  • Enter 'Name' for the iFlow and click 'Add'
  •  

     

  • Now open the artifact you named and click on 'Edit' to start designing.

Step 4: Configure the Sender (HTTPS)

  • Click the 'Sender' and connect it to 'Start (Start Message)'.
  • In the Adapter selection choose 'HTTPS'.
  • Configure in 'Connection'  

  •  

  • Provide 'Address': /first and uncheck 'CSRF Protected' to simplify HTTPS sender adapter setup by allowing direct state-changing requests without the extra token fetch step.

Step 5: Add a Content Modifier to set the response

Now we will create the response body

  • From the palette, drag 'Content Modifier' into the flow.
  • Connect it between 'Start' and 'End'.
  • Click the 'Content Modifier' and go to 'Message Body' tab.
  • In 'Body' write the message. 

Step 6: Save and deploy the iFlow

  • Click 'Save'
  • Click 'Deploy'
  • Deployment publishes the iFlow  to runtime, so it becomes executable.

Step 7: Find the Endpoint URL

After deployment:

  • Go to Monitor / Integrations and APIs
  • Open 'Manage Integration Content'.
  • Select your deployed iFlow
  • Copy the endpoint URL shown for the HTTPS sender

Step 8: Testing your iFlow

To test the iFlow, I used Postman, which is a tool for sending HTTP requests to APIs.

  • Open Postman
  • Click 'New Request'
  • Choose the request method.
  • Paste the iFlow Endpoint URL.
  • Add authentication details if required.
  • Click 'Send'.
  • This will trigger the iFlow.

If everything configured correctly, SAP CPI returns a response:

This confirms that the iFlow is deployed successfully and is able to receive and respond to requests.

Note: If you receive errors such as 401, 403, or 404, verify endpoint URL, deployment status and authentication settings.

Step 9: Monitor Message Processing

To confirm CPI processed your message:

  • Go to Monitor / Integrations and APIs
  • Open 'Monitor Message Processing'
  • Find your 'Artifact Name' and check for 'Status'.
  • Monitoring is where you troubleshoot and prove your iFlow is working in runtime.

Optional: Use 'Trace' for deep debugging

If you want to see payload in each step:

  • Go to 'Manage Integration Content'
  • Select your iFlow --> set Log level = Trace
  • Trigger the iFlow again
  • Go back to message monitor and open 'Trace' view.

Note: Trace expires after a short time and trace data is cleared after a retention period, so use it only during debugging.

Conclusion

Creating your first iFlow in SAP CPI is a great starting point for understanding how integrations work in SAP Integration Suite. Even with a simple example like this, you can gain practical experience in designing, deploying, testing and monitoring an integration flow. Once you are comfortable with these basics, you can move on to more advanced topics such as message transformation, routing, and connecting external systems.

entry.title: Incident Management with AI Adapter and ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction:

With the latest capabilities in SAP Integration Suite, organizations can make incident handling much smarter and faster. By combining the AI Adapter with the ServiceNow Adapter, integration flows can automatically analyze errors and create incident tickets without human intervention.

Instead of developers or support teams manually reviewing logs and raising tickets, Generative AI can interpret the error, understand the issue, and automatically trigger the creation of incident in ServiceNow. This makes the entire exception-handling process more efficient and responsive.

By introducing AI into the integration workflow, organizations can significantly reduce manual effort and lower the Mean Time to Repair (MTTR) for critical integration failures.

 

Disclaimer: This blog includes extensive step-by-step explanations and several screenshots, which may make the article longer than usual. 

 

Concept Overview:

The architecture follows three simple steps:

1. Capture the Error Messages

It’s best to create a generic iflow to handle the entire process of automating the ticket creation in ServiceNow from the error generated in other iflows to be directed using JMS or processDirect adapters

2. Analyze and Generate the Prompt

Using the AI Adapter, the error payload is sent to a Large Language Model (LLM). The model analyzes the stack trace or error message and generates:

  • A simplified explanation of the issue
  • Possible root causes
  • Suggested resolution steps

3. Raise Ticket using ServiceNow Adapter

The enriched information is then sent to the ServiceNow Adapter, which automatically creates a structured incident ticket containing the AI-generated analysis.

AI Adapter Variants:

The AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite currently supports two variants:

  1. AICore-GenAIHub
  2. Custom-OpenAI-REST

AICore-GenAIHub

The AICore-GenAIHub variant integrates with the SAP AI Core service via the SAP Generative AI Hub. SAP GenAI Setup

The Generative AI Hub provides a central gateway to multiple generative AI models from different providers, allowing organizations to consume and manage AI models through SAP’s AI infrastructure on BTP. This enables us to switch between

To use this variant:

  • SAP AI Core service must be configured in the BTP tenant.
  • It supports OAuth2 Client Credentials authentication
  • The service is available only in enterprise or extended environments, not in trial accounts.

Since this setup is not available in the trial landscape, this article will focus on the Custom-OpenAI-REST variant.

Custom-OpenAI-REST

The Custom-OpenAI-REST variant allows integration with AI models that are not hosted or managed by SAP.

This option is used when an AI platform exposes OpenAI-compatible APIs but is not directly supported by SAP’s predefined adapter configurations.

Key characteristics:

  • Configuration Type: Basic or Advanced
  • Connects to OpenAI-compatible endpoints
  • Uses standard REST APIs such as
    • /chat/completions
    • /responses
  • Supports API Key based authentication using Secure Parameters

This enables integration with external AI providers such as OpenAI, Google, or other platforms that expose OpenAI-style APIs.

Prerequisites:

Before building this integration scenario, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  1. Google AI Studio account

    Choose an AI model and generate an API key.

    In this example, we will use the Gemini 1.5 Flash model available via Google AI Studio.

  2. ServiceNow account and Instance

    A ServiceNow account and an active instance are required to create and manage incident tickets through the ServiceNow adapter.

Note: Since I’m using the Gemini AI model, I will configure the adapter using the Advanced configuration type. I will also explain the Basic configuration type and highlight the key differences between the two.

Subscribe to the Gemini Flash Model

First, log in to Google AI Studio and generate an API key that will be used in our IFlow.

After successfully logging in, navigate to the Dashboard section to proceed with the API key creation.

 

Next, go to the Projects section and create a new project by providing an appropriate name. Navigate to the Keys section and create a new key.

Click on the Create API key

Your key has now been generated. Make sure to save it, as it will be required later.

Alternatively, you can click on “Copy cURL quickstart”, which will provide the complete URL, API key, and request body, as shown below.

curl "https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent" \
-H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
-H 'X-goog-api-key: <Your API Key>' \
-X POST \
-d '{
"contents": [
{
"parts": [
{
"text": "Explain how AI works in a few words"
}
]
}
]
}'

ServiceNow Instance Creation:

Now let’s set up the ServiceNow Developer account and create an instance. Visit the ServiceNow Developers page and create an account using your e-mail address. Once you verify your e-mail, you will be redirected to the following screen.

Select the instance of your choice. In my case, I’m selecting the Zurich instance.

Your personal developer instance has now been created, and you will be able to see the generated username and password. We will use these credentials in the ServiceNow Adapter for Basic Authentication

Now that we have the credentials, we need the API details to create an incident in ServiceNow. Log in to your instance using the REST API Explorer | ServiceNow. Once you open it, you will be able to see the list of available resources.

  1. API Name: Select Table API.
  2. Choose the Create a Record (POST) option.
  3. Copy the URL and replace {tablename} with the actual table name (in our case, the incident table).
  4. Scroll down and select the fields that need to be included in the request body.

Note: In ServiceNow, all data is stored in tables. It is also possible to create an empty incident without passing any data in the request body.

As shown below, a ticket is created even when no data is provided in the request body.

To view the list of created incidents, go to Workspaces, search for Incidents, select All Results, and under Service Decks, click on Incidents.

IFlow Process Flow Overview

Finally, let’s take a look at our IFlow and understand how we will use the above setup in our adapters.

Integration Flow

As the Sender, we have used the HTTPS adapter and tested the IFlow using Postman. Based on the requirements, this can be changed - for example, the JMS or ProcessDirect adapters can also be used instead of the HTTPS adapter.

Next comes the key part. In the Content Modifier, I have explicitly defined the prompt that will be sent to the AI model.

AI Model Prompt Content Payload

You can customize the prompt based on your requirements. Now, let’s look at the AI Adapter and the configuration required for the Custom OpenAI REST variant.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Use the base URL of the endpoint.
  • API Key Alias: Specify the secure parameter created in the Security Material. This secure parameter stores the API key for the AI model.

AI Adapter - Connection Settings

In the Processing tab configuration, since I’m not using an OpenAI model like ChatGPT—which provides standard endpoints such as /chat/completions or /responses (we’ll look at this later)—I have selected the Advanced type. This option provides more configuration parameters compared to the Basic type.

  1. HTTP Method – Since we are triggering the process to create an incident, select the POST method.

  2. Relative URL – Copy the relative path from the actual endpoint URL and paste it here:

    /v1beta/models/gemini-flash-latest:generateContent

  3. Query – This is mostly used with the GET method. In our case, it is not applicable, so leave it as the default.

Headers

I created header parameters in the Content Modifier named X-goog-api-key to pass the API key and also added Content-Type: application/json.

Content Modifier Header Properties

Processing Tab:

AI Adapter - Advanced Configuration Type Settings

Let’s also discuss the Basic configuration type.

AI Adapter Basic Configuration Type Setting

 

 Parameter NameDescription
Configuration TypeDetermines how the AI adapter is configured. Basic allows configuration through predefined UI fields such as model and prompt, while Advanced provides more flexibility to define the entire request payload manually.
CategorySpecifies the type of AI capability to be used. Available options include Chat Completions, Responses, and Platform. Each category represents a different set of AI APIs used for conversational AI, structured responses, or advanced platform capabilities such as embeddings and image generation.
Entity TypeRepresents the specific AI resource within the selected category. 
Operation

Defines the action to be executed using the selected entity. The available operations depend on the selected Category and Entity Type.

For example, in Chat Completions, the operation is typically Create a model response for a given conversation. Other entities in the platform category may support operations like create, retrieve, update, or delete resources.

Request Payload Source

Determines how the request payload (prompt and parameters) is passed to the AI model.

Options include:

1. UI Configurable – Allows the prompt and model to be defined directly in the adapter configuration.

2. Exchange Body – Allows the request payload to be dynamically provided from the integration flow, typically using a Content Modifier or Groovy script, and passed as the message body.

Model (when UI Configurable is selected)

Specifies the AI model used to generate the response. The list contains the models available in the configured AI service (for example, GPT-based models).

Prompt (when UI Configurable is selected)

Allows the user to directly define the prompt or instruction that will be sent to the AI model. It can include instructions, questions, or contextual information required for the AI interaction.

Difference between /chat/completions and /response endpoints in OpenAI API

Feature/chat/completions/responses

Purpose

Designed specifically for chat-based interactionsA unified endpoint for all types of AI responses
Input FormatUses messages array (system, user, assistant)Uses a more flexible input structure
Future scopeOlder approach (still supported)Recommended modern API by OpenAI
FunctionalitiesPrimarily text/chatSupports text, images, audio, and tool use

Example:

1. /chat/completions

POST /v1/chat/completions
{
  "model":"gpt-4o-mini",
  "messages": [
    {"role":"system", "content":"You are a helpful assistant."},
    {"role":"user", "content":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"}
  ]
}

2. /responses

POST /v1/responses
{
  "model":"gpt-4.1-mini",
  "input":"Explain SAP CPI in simple terms"
}

Key Takeaway

  • /chat/completions → Built specifically for chat-style conversations.
  • /responses → A new unified API that works for multiple AI capabilities and is the recommended approach for new integrations.

As the next step, I created a Groovy script to process the response from the AI and extract and format the relevant content required. If you look at my prompt, I asked the model to return the response in JSON format. However, inside the Content tag, the model returned something like this:

{
  "candidates": [
    {
      "content": {
        "parts": [
          {
            "text": "```json\n{\n  \"ErrorDescription\": \"Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.\",\n  \"PossibleCauses\": \"Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.\",\n  \"ResolutionSteps\": \"Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.\",\n  \"SupportGuidance\": \"If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.\",\n  \"IncidentMetadata\": {\n    \"state\": \"In Progress\",\n    \"incident_state\": \"Investigating\",\n    \"impact\": \"2\"\n  }\n}\n```"
          }
        ],
        "role": "model"
      },
      "finishReason": "STOP",
      "index": 0
    }
  ],
  "usageMetadata": {
    "promptTokenCount": 257,
    "candidatesTokenCount": 266,
    "totalTokenCount": 1366,
    "promptTokensDetails": [
      {
        "modality": "TEXT",
        "tokenCount": 257
      }
    ],
    "thoughtsTokenCount": 843
  },
  "modelVersion": "gemini-2.5-flash",
  "responseId": "XvevabT6KcDcqtsP_8GP8Q0"
}

Next, I used a Groovy script to extract only the JSON content present inside the text tag. The output after the Groovy script will contain only the required JSON content. Now, we can use the JSON to XML Converter to transform the below payload into an XML structure.

{
  "ErrorDescription": "Access denied due to missing or invalid authentication credentials for the requested resource.",
  "PossibleCauses": "Invalid or incorrect username/password was provided; The authentication token or API key is missing; The provided token has expired; The user account is locked or disabled; Insufficient permissions for accessing the resource; Server-side authentication configurations are incorrect; Network issues prevented sending valid credentials.",
  "ResolutionSteps": "Verify your username and password are correct; Ensure the authentication token or API key is present and valid; Generate a new authentication token if it has expired; Confirm you are using the correct authentication method; Contact your administrator to check account status and permissions; Review server logs for authentication configuration issues; Check network connectivity to the authentication provider; Clear your browser cache and cookies, then retry the action.",
  "SupportGuidance": "If the issue persists after following the resolution steps, please contact your IT support team. Provide them with the exact error message, time of occurrence, and any steps you have already attempted. This information will help expedite the investigation.",
  "IncidentMetadata": {
    "state": "In Progress",
    "incident_state": "Investigating",
    "impact": "2"
  }
}

In the next Content Modifier, I created a few properties with the Source Type set to XPath. These properties are then used to construct the request payload that will be sent to ServiceNow.

Custom Properties created in Content Modifier

Content Modifier Request Payload for ServiceNow Ticket Creation

Content Modifier Body:

{
    "short_description": "${property.short_description}",
    "description": "${property.PossibleCauses}, ${property.ResolutionSteps}, ${property.SupportGuidance}",
    "state": "${property.state}",
    "incident_state": "${property.incident_state}",
    "impact": "${property.impact}"
}

Finally, let’s look at the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter.

In the Connection tab:

  • Address: Copy and paste your ServiceNow instance URL.
  • Authentication: Select Basic Authentication.
  • Credentials: Enter the user credentials that you created in the Security Material.

ServiceNow Connection Tab Settings

In the Processing tab, we are presented with several additional parameters to configure.

ServiceNow Adapter Processing Tab Configurations

API Version - You can choose from different REST endpoint versions. If you are not certain which version to use, it is recommended to leave it as the default value.

Operation - Several operations are available. Since we are creating an incident, select the Create Object operation.

Table Name - Incident because that were all the ticket are stored in ServiceNow

Display Values - The Display Value option determines how reference field values are returned in the response payload. 

In ServiceNow tables, many fields store internal system identifiers (Sys IDs) instead of human-readable values. The Display Value setting controls whether the adapter returns:

  • the raw system value,
  • the human-readable display value, or
  • both the system value and display value.

Access Data Across Domain - This option allows the adapter to retrieve records from multiple domains within a ServiceNow instance.

  • If disabled, the adapter accesses data only from the current domain.

  • If enabled, it can retrieve records from all available domains, such as Global, IT, or Infrastructure.

Response Field - Specifies the fields or properties to be returned in the ServiceNow response. If multiple fields are required, they should be separated using commas.

Render Response For - Available options are Both, Mobile, and Desktop. This setting determines how the response is formatted based on the client type, ensuring compatibility across different platforms.

Suppress System Fields Auto Generation - This option prevents ServiceNow from automatically generating certain system fields when a record is created. Examples include: sys_id, sys_created_on

Display Raw Values - When Display Raw Values is enabled, the API returns the exact value stored in the database. By default, ServiceNow returns data in a formatted or human-readable format.

Exclude Reference Link - In ServiceNow responses, reference fields often include a link to the related resource. When Exclude Reference Link is enabled, the response returns only the value and removes the reference link.

Let’s now look at the actual difference in the response when all these properties are enabled.

Response Without Enabling the Mentioned Fields

Response when the Mentioned Fields are enabled

This is how these options affect the ServiceNow response payload.

Under the Format section, we have Request and Response options. These are quite straightforward, so I’ll skip a detailed explanation here.

Note: The available properties may vary depending on the selected operation. For more details about the ServiceNow Receiver Adapter, refer to the official documentation. ServiceNow Receiver Adapter | SAP Help Portal

Important Note: Make sure your ServiceNow instance is active and running. If the instance is in sleep mode while testing the IFlow, you may receive a response such as “Instance Hibernating Page.”

End to End testing from Postman

In the first Content Modifier, the error tag is captured and stored in a property. In the message body, the expression ${property.errorMsg} is used so that the prompt dynamically includes the specific error content. This ensures that for each error, the AI model receives a different prompt based on the error message.

Response: 

You can see an incident is created with a number "INC0010027". Now, let’s check ServiceNow to see where the incident is created. As expected, a new entry appears in the incident table.

Conclusion:

By integrating the AI Adapter with ServiceNow in SAP Integration Suite, we can significantly improve how incidents are identified and created within enterprise landscapes. Instead of manually analyzing logs and raising tickets, the integration flow can capture errors, send them to an AI model for analysis, and automatically generate a structured incident in ServiceNow.

entry.title: Introducing SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition
entry.content:
Why Integration Suite Is Now the Most Strategic Investment for Your AI and Agentic AI Future 

Across every industry, companies are racing to adopt AI. However, the reality is that many organizations are experimenting with models, copilots, and prototypes, with just a smaller number managing to scale AI into real business operations. The reason for the wide gap between experimentation and enterprise level adoption is trust. Now, with SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition, Agentic AI implementations can move from analyzing and experimenting to confidently acting. 

It’s a foundational shift in how businesses can safely, securely, and efficiently bring AI into the core of their operations. SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition provides access to newly released AI features. These AI features include capabilities aimed at improving developer productivity and features that address operational efficiency.  

SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition feature set 

Developer productivity features include:  

  • Text-to-Flow generation helps developers build integration flows using a natural language interface. 
  • Script Optimization enables developers to analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript scripts used inside iFlows within SAP Integration Suite’s Cloud Integration component. 

Operations efficiency features include:  

  • API Anomaly Detection provides customers with AI-driven observability and monitoring by detecting unusual behavior in message processing and identifying integration issues early.  
  • API Traffic Prediction leverages AI-augmented design and governance capabilities within the API Management component, to assist developers and architects in discovering, designing, and optimizing APIs more intelligently. 

In addition to these powerful AI-orientated Integration features, SAP Integration Suite enhanced edition also provides customers with access to: 

  • The entire suite of integration capabilities: application-to-application integration, business-to-business integration, end-to-end API Management, edge integration capabilities, and hundreds of 3rd party connectors.  
  • Integration Suite, advanced event mesh for event driven architecture-based use cases. 
  • SAP Document AI converts unstructured data into structured data  
  • Alert Notification Service proactively alerts users about system events, anomalies, or errors across iFlows, APIs, and adapters. 
  • Transport Management Service moves integration artifacts and configurations across BTP subaccounts and landscapes without direct system access or manual export/import. 

Below, we break down why this release is so important and how it will help your organization accelerate into an AI-powered future. 

AI Needs Access. Agentic AI Needs Action. Integration Suite Enables Both. 

AI models are only as good as the data they can reach. Agentic AI goes a step further: it requires secure, governed pathways to take actions across systems—from creating a purchase order, to updating a vendor record, to executing a workflow. 

SAP Integration Suite provides: 

  • More Unified, governed connectivity to SAP and non-SAP systems 
  • Real-time event streams and APIs that support autonomous agent actions 
  • Standardized data models for consistent, reliable AI reasoning 
  • Workflow orchestration that ensures agents can complete end-to-end processes 

In other words: 
Integration Suite becomes the digital circulatory system that allows AI and Agentic AI to interact safely and intelligently with your valuable enterprise process and application data sources 

Why This Release Matters for Customers 

1. You Enable AI That Actually Works Across Your Enterprise Landscape 

Most companies run dozens—sometimes hundreds—of interconnected systems. Traditional AI pilots struggle because they operate in isolation. Integration Suite provides: 

  • Prebuilt connectors to your critical business systems 
  • Harmonized, real-time data flows 
  • A way to eliminate integration debt that slows down AI initiatives 

Your AI doesn’t stay stuck in a lab. It connects to your business. 

 2. You Unlock Agentic AI Capabilities Faster and More Safely 

Agentic AI can execute actions as directed, but enterprises require strict governance. Enhanced edition provides access controls, auditability, error handling, and regulates usage which allows companies to facilitate AI agents requests knowing that every action is secured, monitored, and fully reversible. 

 3. You Free Your Teams from Integration Bottlenecks 

Every AI use case requires connectivity. Before now, that meant long waits for custom integrations. 
With SAP Integration Suite: 

  • APIs, events, and integration flows become reusable assets 
  • Low-code tooling accelerates experimentation 
  • Citizen developers and integration teams get a shared workspace 

This means you can support AI use cases quickly when your business needs them. 

 4. You Prepare Your Landscape for a Hybrid, AI-Driven Future 

AI doesn’t just live in the cloud. Many mission-critical workloads remain on-premise or in private environments. Integration Suite ensures: 

  • AI can work across all your landscapes 
  • Legacy systems don’t become barriers to innovation 
  • You can adopt new AI capabilities without restructuring your architecture 

Supporting where your business runs today, maintaining ful compatibility with where AI is going tomorrow. 

 5. You Establish a Foundation for Scalable, Repeatable AI Value 

Each new integration flow, each API, each event—becomes part of a reusable catalog.  
Over time, your organization builds a growing library of AI-ready building blocks that accelerate every future use case. 

This creates compounding value, year after year. 

What This Means for Your Business Today 

With the enhancements in this release, SAP Integration Suite becomes more than a technical enabler — it becomes your Enterprise circulatory system And, most importantly It becomes the key accelerator that turns AI ideas into enterprise-grade, production-ready outcomes. 

What’s Next? 

This release is a major step in SAP’s broader vision of building an enterprise-ready AI ecosystem where: 

  • Data flows freely but securely 
  • Systems speak the same language 
  • AI operates with governance and trust 
  • Autonomous agents can navigate complex end-to-end processes 

We’re excited to see how you apply these new capabilities to unlock efficiency, fuel innovation, and reshape how work gets done. 

 

More details can be found at:

Discovery Center

SAP Note  - Service Plans and Upgrade Paths 

SAP Integration Suite Roadmap

entry.title: Key Skills Every SAP Consultant Needs in 2026
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP consultants can no longer focus on one tool. Projects now demand broader technical ownership. Clients expect speed, clarity, and stable systems. This requires strong core skills and smart choices.

1. Strong S/4HANA Technical Foundation

Deep S/4HANA knowledge is mandatory. This includes data model changes and CDS views. Understand embedded analytics and Fiori architecture. Know where custom code still makes sense.

2. Clean Core Understanding

Clean core is no longer optional. Custom code must be reduced and controlled. Know what belongs in S/4HANA. Know what belongs outside the core. Follow SAP guidance, not shortcuts. Shortcuts always create future issues.

3. SAP BTP Hands-on Experience

SAP BTP skills are critical in 2026. Extensions move away from the core system. Know CAP, RAP, and side-by-side patterns. Understand event-driven and API-based designs. You must build, not just explain.

4. Integration Knowledge

Most SAP systems do not work alone. Integration skills are now core skills. Understand SAP Integration Suite basics. Know IDocs, APIs, and event messaging. Debugging integration issues is a daily task.

5. ABAP Skills That Still Matter

ABAP is not dead. Bad ABAP is. Know modern ABAP syntax. Avoid outdated patterns and heavy reports. Write clean, readable, testable code.

6. Fiori and UX Awareness

Users judge systems by screens. Fiori is no longer optional. Understand standard apps before custom ones. Avoid rebuilding what SAP already provides. Simple UX reduces training and errors.

7. Security and Authorizations

Security issues stop projects instantly. Technical consultants must understand roles. Know basic authorization objects. Understand transport and access risks. Never treat security as an afterthought.

8. Automation and Tools

Manual work does not scale. Automation saves time and reduces errors. Use ATC, code inspector, and test tools. Learn basic scripting where useful. Tools make good consultants faster.

9. Communication with Non-Technical Teams

Technical skill alone is not enough. You must explain problems clearly. Use simple words with business users. Avoid technical terms unless required. Clear communication builds trust quickly.

10. Learning Discipline

SAP changes every year. Skills expire faster than before. Read SAP notes and blogs often. Practice in real systems, not slides.

Learning must be continuous and active.

Conclusion

SAP consultants in 2026 must be builders. They must also be careful decision-makers. Strong basics, clean design, and clear thinking matter most.

Trends change. Core skills stay.

entry.title: How AI and Machine Learning Are Quietly Transforming SAP Integrations — My Perspective as a Beginner
entry.content:
The Gap I Noticed in Traditional Integration
In a typical iFlow I built recently, I had to manually define rules like:
∙ If amount is greater than 100000 — route to high value channel
∙ If region is not IN or US — mark as invalid
∙ If quantity is zero — reject the item
These rules work fine when business logic is simple and stable. But what happens when the rules keep changing? What if the threshold for “high value” changes every quarter? What if new regions get added every month? A human has to go in, update the Groovy script, redeploy, test again. That is time consuming and error prone.
This is exactly where AI and ML start making sense in SAP integrations.

 

Where AI/ML Actually Fits in SAP

1. Intelligent Data Mapping

“Mail Based Batch Processing iFlow — routing logic manually defined for each invoice type” 

One of the most frustrating parts of SAP CPI for a beginner is Message Mapping. You have to manually connect every source field to every target field. For simple scenarios this is manageable. But imagine mapping two enterprise systems with hundreds of fields each.
SAP has been working on AI assisted mapping where the system suggests field mappings based on field names, data types and historical mapping patterns. Instead of manually dragging every line, the AI proposes the mapping and you just review and approve. This saves enormous time in large integration projects.

This is my data in Postman


2. Anomaly Detection in Integration Flows

“Manually setting threshold values in Content Modifier — AI could dynamically adjust these thresholds based on patterns”

Imagine your iFlow processes 10000 invoices every day. Suddenly one day 200 invoices fail. Is that normal? Is something broken? Did a source system change its format?
ML models trained on historical message processing logs can detect these anomalies automatically. Instead of waiting for someone to notice failures in the monitor, the system alerts you proactively. SAP Integration Advisor and SAP Alert Notification Service are already moving in this direction.


3. Predictive Error Handling

“Manually defined routing conditions in SAP CPI Router — AI could learn and suggest these rules automatically based on historical data”

Traditional error handling in CPI is reactive — something fails, you get an alert, you investigate. ML changes this to proactive. By analyzing patterns in past failures — which steps fail most often, at what times, with what kind of payloads — a model can predict which messages are likely to fail before they actually do and reroute them automatically.


4. Natural Language to iFlow

“Custom business logic written manually in Groovy — imagine describing this in plain English and having AI generate it automatically” 

This one genuinely surprised me when I read about it. Researchers and SAP teams are experimenting with systems where you describe what you want in plain English — something like “receive invoices from vendor system, validate amounts, send high value ones to finance team via email” — and the system automatically generates a working iFlow.
We are not fully there yet but with large language models becoming more capable this is not science fiction anymore.


5. AI Powered Document Understanding
In my Scenario 3 assignment I processed invoices that were already structured XML. But in the real world invoices come as PDFs, scanned images, emails and all kinds of unstructured formats. SAP Document Information Extraction — part of SAP AI Business Services — uses ML to read these unstructured documents and extract fields like invoice number, vendor name, amount and date automatically. This extracted data can then flow directly into an SAP CPI iFlow for further processing.

 

What SAP Is Actually Building
SAP has been investing heavily in an umbrella platform called SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad. These are the infrastructure layers that allow AI models to be trained, deployed and consumed across SAP systems.
On top of that, specific AI services have been released:
∙ SAP Document Information Extraction — extracts data from unstructured documents
∙ SAP Business Entity Recognition — identifies business entities in text
∙ SAP Translation Hub — ML powered translation for global deployments
∙ SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation — combines RPA with ML for smarter automation
All of these can be connected to SAP Integration Suite, meaning your iFlows can call these AI services as external APIs and enrich your integration logic with intelligence.

A Small Real World Example
Let me paint a picture. Suppose a company receives invoices from 50 different vendors. Each vendor sends invoices in a slightly different format — different field names, different structures, different currencies.
Without AI:
∙ A developer manually creates one mapping per vendor
∙ 50 vendors means 50 mappings to maintain
∙ Every time a vendor changes their format someone has to update the mapping
With AI:
∙ ML model learns the pattern of each vendor’s format from historical data
∙ Automatically adapts mappings when formats change slightly
∙ Flags only major structural changes for human review
∙ Developer effort reduces from weeks to hours

 

My Honest Take


I am still a beginner with SAP CPI. I am still figuring out XPath expressions and Groovy scripts. But even at this level I can see that the manual rule based approach has limits. The more I work with integration flows the more I appreciate why AI is being brought into this space.
The integrations of tomorrow will not just move data — they will understand it, validate it intelligently, predict problems before they happen and adapt to changes automatically. SAP is clearly building towards that future and I think it is genuinely exciting for anyone working in this space today.

References and Further Reading
∙ SAP AI Business Services on SAP Discovery Center
∙ SAP Integration Advisor Documentation
∙ SAP AI Core on SAP Help Portal
∙ SAP Community blogs on Intelligent Integration

 This blog reflects my personal observations and learning while working with SAP Integration Suite as part of my coursework. All scenarios mentioned are based on hands-on assignment work.

entry.title: Ask Me Anything with SAP Champions – Monthly Series (Session 2)
entry.content:
Hey dear SAP Community,

My name is Maria Antonieta Lopez,  I currently work as an SAP program director focusing on global SAP transformation programs, SAP S/4HANA implementations, enterprise architecture, governance frameworks, and digital innovation within the SAP ecosystem.

As an SAP Champion myself, I’ll be leading the March edition for the Ask Me Anything series, which we started together with my dear friend Sebastiano Marchesini.

So yes…

You can ask. Me. ANYTHING.

After 20+ years living inside SAP transformations, I’ve probably seen the good, the bad… and the “how did this system survive go-live?” moments. 😅

So over the next weeks I’ll be answering any question you have -- No filters - Just real SAP Live --.

💡We can explore topics like:

⚙️SAP S/4HANA implementations and global transformation programs. - How to successfully deliver end-to-end SAP S/4HANA implementations and large-scale transformation programs, enabling organizations to modernize their core business processes, standardize global operations, and adopt next-generation digital capabilities.

🏗️ SAP Enterprise Architecture. - Processes, artifacts, and strategies for TOGAF-based SAP enterprise architecture.

🧼 Clean Core in SAP Cloud ERP (Private & Public). - Project management processes for implementing or creating extensions or interfaces in your project. (BUILD for configurators and its use with Joule, pre-built APIs, newly developed APIs, and, of course, our platform-as-a-service, SAP Integration Suite, obviously within our beloved SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP).

☁️ RISE or GROW with SAP + Clean Core Governance & Methodologies. - From SAP Activate  to frameworks such as SAP Application Extension Methodology, Integration Solution Advisory Methodology, SAP Design Thinking, SAP Data Architecture Framework, and why not… even the SAP Business AI Framework.

We can talk about which format to use, or the process for something, or even… why I have to do this task.... and I'll tell you what will happen if you don't...

Muahaha! …💀 soo Yes… my SAP horror stories - like winter - are coming…

🏛️ How to build an SAP Center of Excellence (CoE) or provide an SAP AM services and actually 💙 live happily ever after with SAP 💙 using Cloud ALM + Focused Build + Activate.

🌍Globalization in a nutshell or by country. To comply locally and succeed globally... Long live SAP Document & Reporting Compliance! And our SAP partners' DRC extensions.

🧭 Solution roadmaps, etc.

And... if I don't know the answer? 👉 I promise I'll find someone who does... for you.

 

We have everything ready...  💬 Now it’s your turn.

Drop your question in the comments section 👇or You can ask anonymously here 👉 AMA Champion Anonymously Questions  or Scanning this QR 

 

This AMA edition will be open for questions until March 27, 2026.

After that, selected questions will be answered in a recorded video published on the SAP Community YouTube channel  👉https://www.youtube.com/@SAPCommunity 
A blog recap summarizing the questions and answers will also be published for the community.

Cuentame…

🤓What SAP topic should we explore together? ESPAÑOL or ENGLISH

entry.title: Modernizing integrations with event‑driven architecture and real customer use cases for SAP AEM
entry.content:
Customers want real-time business.  But many integration landscapes still rely on rigid, point-to-point connections that slow everything down.

So what’s the alternative?  Event-driven architecture.

In this upcoming session, we’ll explore how SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh (AEM) enables applications to communicate through events, creating flexible, scalable connections across SAP and non-SAP systems.

And we won’t stay theoretical.  You’ll see real customer scenarios showing how AEM helps distribute data instantly, reduce integration dependencies, and keep processes running smoothly across complex landscapes.

Plus, we’ll share practical guidance for identifying where event-driven approaches make sense in customer architectures, something partners are increasingly being asked about.

If you’re working with customers on modern integration strategies, this session is packed with insights you can immediately apply.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/mar/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013314.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #EventDrivenArchitecture #ModernIntegration #SAPIntegrationSuite

entry.title: Mapping SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) Adapter to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ Adapter
entry.content:
This blog post is part of the series Master Blog: SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tool and Guidance exploring how to migrate various integration patterns from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite using the Migration Tool.

Objective of this Blog

In this blog, we will focus on the mapping of SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter — with Transport Protocol “WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider” and Message Protocol both “JMS 1.x” or “JMS 2.0”) — to SAP Integration Suite IBM MQ adapter.

Since the Migration Tool skips the SAP Process Orchestration JMS (IBM MQ) adapter, the approach described in this blog will help to manually configure both Sender and Receiver IBM MQ adapters in SAP Integration Suite iFlows based on source JMS (IBM MQ) SAP Process Orchestration scenarios.

Related Blogs 

  • IBM MQ JMS Adapter for SAP Integration Suite
  • Integrating IBM MQ On-Premise with SAP Cloud Integration Using the IBM MQ Adapter

SAP Process Orchestration JMS Adapter Modules

In SAP Process Orchestration, the following JMS Adapter Modules are commonly used to convert message payloads between binary and XML formats:                       

  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertJMSMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertBinaryToXMBMessage
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/ConvertMessageToBinary
  • SAP XI JMS Adapter/SendBinarytoXIJMSService

In SAP Process Orchestration, these modules are often required when integrating with JMS providers such as IBM MQ. However, in SAP Integration Suite they are not required, as their functionality is provided directly by the IBM MQ adapter itself. The IBM MQ adapter in SAP Integration Suite offers built-in options for message conversion, allowing users to select either byte or text message formats directly in the adapter configuration.

Variants

Based on the SAP Process Orchestration source parameters, the migration will result in three different target variants:

- Sender

  • JMS Poll
  • JMS Subscribe

- Receiver:

  • JMS

Sender

The source Sender JMS MQ channel must have the following properties:

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Sender
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Sender variants — JMS Poll and JMS Subscribe — are detailed below (REST variant doesn’t apply).

JMS Poll

JMS Poll implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter actively polls messages from IBM MQ queues or topics at defined intervals, using a request-based approach to check for new messages periodically.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view not checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

 

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Process Orchestration

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite

SAP Process Orchestration

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Source view

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “WebSphereMQ Cipher Spec”:

If “Use SSL” checked and “WSMQ Cipher Spec” = “Other”:

Processing view

SAP Integration Suite

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:

Connection view

* After maintaining the Credentials in the SAP Integration Suite Security Materials section, the alias name must be provided in the Credential Name field to establish the connection.

Processing view

If “Message Destination” = “Topic”:

If “Message Destination” = “Queue”:

 JMS Subscribe

JMS Subscribe implements a message retrieval mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Sender Adapter establishes an active subscription to IBM MQ queues or topics, enabling real-time or near real-time message delivery as soon as messages are published to the destination.

Condition

In SAP Process Orchestration, for this variant to apply, the following condition must be met:

“Use Message Listener Based Connector” checkbox in the Processing view checked:

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

Same mapping as for the JMS Poll variant, but without fields 14, 15, and 16.

Receiver

The source Receiver JMS MQ channel must have the following properties: 

  • Adapter Type: JMS
  • Receiver
  • Transport Protocol: WebSphereMQ (MQSeries) JMS Provider
  • Message protocol: JMS 1.x or JMS 2.0

The relevant Receiver variant — JMS — is detailed below (HTTPS variant doesn’t apply):

JMS

JMS Receiver implements a message publishing mechanism where the IBM MQ JMS Receiver Adapter enables SAP Integration Suite to send messages to IBM MQ queues or topics, acting as a message producer that publishes data to designated destinations in the IBM MQ server.

Mapping (SAP Process Orchestration → SAP Integration Suite)

The following table shows the mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite:

The following SAP Process Orchestration screenshots illustrate the mapping:

The following SAP Integration Suite screenshots illustrate the mapping:
